places.name: Asuncion

visits.text: Asunción's historic center unfolds on foot from Plaza de Armas, the colonial heart of the capital founded in 1537. Dominating the square stands the Cabildo—the former seat of government now transformed into the cultural center of the Republic—worth visiting as much for its architecture as for its permanent exhibitions on Paraguay's history. The Catedral Metropolitana, on the opposite side of the plaza, ranks among South America's oldest cathedrals; take time to walk around the square rather than driving through it, as the atmosphere shifts dramatically depending on the hour.

The Panteón Nacional de los Héroes stands as Paraguay's most symbolically charged building. Commissioned in October 1863 by President Francisco Solano López as an oratory to the Virgin, designed by Italian architect Alessandro Ravizza, it remained unfinished for over 70 years because of the War of the Triple Alliance—the most devastating conflict in South American history, which killed between 60 and 90 percent of Paraguay's male population. Only in 1936, after the Chaco War, did Colonel Rafael Franco have the building completed and declared it a national mausoleum. You'll see the tombs of Mariscal Francisco Solano López, his father Carlos Antonio López, and Chaco War hero José Félix Estigarribia. Practical detail: the building also serves as kilometer zero for all Paraguayan national routes—eight major highways radiate from here.

The Palacio de López ranks among the most romantic constructions in colonial America. Francisco Solano López began its construction in 1857 on land inherited from his godfather, in neoclassical style tinged with Palladian influences, under the direction of English architect Alonso Taylor. Materials came from all over Paraguay: stone from Emboscada quarries, timber from Yaguarón, bricks from Tacumbú. In 1868, the Brazilian fleet bombarded it from the river and Allied forces ransacked it—European furniture, statues, and mirrors were carted off to Brazil. López never lived there. Admire it from the Costanera esplanade, river side: the view of the illuminated Palacio at sunset, with the Paraguay River below, is the finest in the city.

The Museo del Barro—officially Centro de Artes Visuales—is undoubtedly Paraguay's most singular museum. Founded in 1979 by Carlos Colombino, Ticio Escobar, and Osvaldo Salerno during the Stroessner dictatorship, it brings together three collections under one roof: peasant folk art (over 4,000 pieces including 17th-18th century Franciscan and Jesuit carvings), indigenous art (over 2,500 objects from all the country's ethnic groups—ceramics, feather ornaments, Kamba Ra'anga masks), and contemporary Ibero-American art (around 3,000 works). What makes this place unique is its choice to present these three art forms on equal footing, without hierarchy. Allow at least 90 minutes to appreciate it—entry is free on Fridays and Saturdays.

The Jardín Botánico y Zoológico of Asunción occupies the former López family estate in the northern part of the city. While the zoo section isn't its strong suit, the historic López greenhouses—built in the late 19th century—and tree-lined avenues with century-old specimens make the visit worthwhile as a shaded rest between museums. The botanical section preserves a fine collection of Paraguayan palms and subtropical species.

The Manzana de la Rivera comprises seven restored 19th-century colonial houses aligned along El Paraguayo Independiente street facing the Palacio de López. Each house represents a different architectural period—from colonial dwelling to fin-de-siècle bourgeois mansion. Inside, the cultural center hosts temporary exhibitions on Paraguayan history and arts. It's one of the rare coherent urban sequences of the city's old colonial fabric: don't pass by without entering.

Mercado Municipal N°4 is Asunción's most popular covered market and one of the best places to dive into everyday Paraguayan life. Vendors of manioc, peppers, fresh cheese, passion fruit, and chicha have rubbed shoulders here since dawn. Arrive early in the morning to see the activity at its peak—after 10 AM the crowds disperse. It's also the cheapest place in town for breakfast: a hot chipá and a tereré to start the day right.

The Iglesia de la Encarnación, in the neighborhood of the same name, is one of the city's oldest parishes. Its sober white architecture contrasts with the bustle of the surrounding Villa Morra shopping district—a neighborhood in full transformation where local designer boutiques sit alongside trendy restaurants.

Thirty kilometers from Asunción via Route 2, Itauguá is the world capital of ñandutí—Paraguayan spider-lace. The Guaraní word literally means "tela de araña," spider web, and perfectly describes these discs of thread woven on wooden frames in radiating circles, imitating a web's geometry. The craft isn't of pure Guaraní origin: it derives from a Spanish lace from the Canary Islands, the "soles de Tenerife," introduced to Paraguay during colonization and transformed by mestiza women until it became something unique. In 2019, it was declared Intangible Cultural Heritage of the Nation. Enter the artisans' workshops—most downtown houses have their studios open to the street—and watch the tejedoras work: a single piece can take several days depending on its size and the complexity of the patterns.

Atyrá, founded in 1580 under the name "aty-há" (gathering place) by Franciscans Luis de Bolaños and Alonso de San Buenaventura, is one of the first Franciscan reductions of the Río de la Plata. This flowery Cordillera village preserves its 18th-century church and central plaza typical of the period, with polychrome altarpieces and carved images in the Hispanic-Guaraní baroque style. The atmosphere is more authentic than in Itauguá: fewer shops, more neighborhood life.

On the return road to Asunción, plan a stop in Tobatí in the Cordillera department. This Franciscan village founded in 1583 by Luis de Bolaños is one of the cradles of Guaraní-Franciscan culture: the colonial church preserves polychrome wood carvings and the central plaza with its historic well gives a fine idea of the urban planning of the reductions. It's also here that, according to legend, one of the two Virgin statues carved by the Guaraní Indian José was born—the big sister of the Virgencita de Caacupé.

Arriving in Asunción in late afternoon, take a tour of the Recoleta neighborhood in the north of the center, which the itinerary hasn't yet covered. Its tree-lined avenues, art galleries, and lively plaza make it one of the capital's most pleasant neighborhoods to settle into after the drive. The Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes, housed in a 19th-century villa, is worth a quick look if you still have energy.

Cerro Lambaré is the hill that dominates the southern suburbs of Asunción, river side. From the mirador at the summit, on clear days, you see on one side the urban panorama of Asunción spread out to the Palacio de López, and on the other the meanders of the Río Paraguay disappearing toward the Chaco. It's not a very developed tourist site—no cafés or shops—which makes it a good place for quiet sunrises and sunsets.

The Iglesia Santísima Trinidad in the Trinidad neighborhood, northeast of downtown Asunción, is a beautiful neighborhood church with a sober baroque facade and an interior decorated with 18th-century wood carvings. It's also the name of one of the capital's liveliest neighborhoods, where restaurants and bars cluster—a good base for dinner.

Piribebuy—"fresh air" in Guaraní, because of its numerous streams—is one of Paraguay's most history-laden towns. Founded in 1636 by captain-general Martín Ledesma de Valderrama, it became the third provisional capital of the Republic during the War of the Triple Alliance. On August 12, 1869, an Allied army of 20,000 men besieged the town defended by only 1,600 soldiers, women, and children. Commander Pedro Pablo Caballero, summoned to surrender, replied: "I am here to fight and die if necessary, but not to surrender." Paraguayan cannons were loaded with broken glass and stones for lack of ammunition. The Dulce Nombre de Jesús church—consecrated in 1753—was the theater of the final resistance. The adjacent historical museum presents weapons and objects found in the village soil during construction work. Allow 30 minutes for the church and 30 minutes for the museum.

The Centro Cultural de la República El Cabildo, on Plaza de Armas, is the former seat of colonial government and one of the rare 18th-century constructions still standing in the capital. Its temporary exhibitions on Paraguayan political, cultural, and artistic history offer a different perspective on the country—often more critical and less official than that of the national museums. Check the program in advance on social media.

Barrio Sajonia, in the east of downtown Asunción, is one of the city's liveliest working-class neighborhoods, with murals and street art spanning dozens of facades. Founded by German and Italian immigrants in the 19th century, the neighborhood preserves some beautiful houses from that era alongside modern buildings. Wander on foot between Oliva street and Avenida Colón.

Depending on your flight time, one last walk in the center or at the Jardín Botánico is in order. The Jardín Botánico is the quietest place in the city at dawn—hundreds of tropical birds wake up in the trees and loros are visible from the earliest hours. If you're more in town, walk around Plaza de Armas in the morning when it still belongs to Asuncenos and not to tourists.

activities.text: Climb up to the Loma San Jerónimo neighborhood for an entirely different image of Asunción. Perched on a hill above the river, this old working-class barrio is one of the city's oldest: its narrow lanes, ochre and turquoise houses, and stairways that open onto unexpected panoramas of the river make it one of the capital's best street art spots. Go in late afternoon—the golden light on the facades and the view down to the Palacio de López below are worth all the postcards.

In late afternoon, head to the Costanera de Asunción for a sunset stroll. This redeveloped riverfront runs along Bahía de Asunción for several kilometers: Asuncenos gather here with their families, tereré and street food vendors set up from 5 PM onward, and the view of the illuminated Palacio de López from the river side is one of the city's finest. Arrive around 6 PM to settle in before the sky turns orange.

Avenida Palma is Asunción's historic pedestrian boulevard, lined with shopping arcades and craft boutiques. Here you'll find the best selections of ao po'i—the fine embroidered cloth characteristic of Paraguay—and ñandutí, the spider-lace of Guaraní craftsmanship. Prices are more negotiable than at the airport, especially if you seek out small family shops rather than tourist chains.

End the day with a tasting of tereré and Paraguayan street food. Tereré is the cold infusion of yerba mate with medicinal herbs: it's both the national drink and a social ritual you'll see everywhere. Accompany it with sopa paraguaya—despite its name, it's a corn flour and cheese cake, not a soup—and empanadas de carne. The many kiosks along the Costanera or in the center's plazas are the best places for this experience.

The return to Asunción via the scenic Cordillera route—the "Guaraní hills" that stretch between Asunción and Caacupé—is one of the country's most beautiful drives. The red sandstone hills contrast with subtropical vegetation, estancias alternate with small white villages, and views regularly open onto the plain stretching to the Río Paraguay. Take the opportunity to stop at a local farm and buy artisanal cheese or caña paraguaya.

The RN5/RN3 road from Concepción to Asunción crosses some of Paraguay's most authentic rural landscapes: Cordillera hills declining into red earth valleys, estancias with wooden fences, zebu herds on dry pastures in the dry season. Count on about 5 hours of driving for 330 km—leave early in the morning to arrive in Asunción in the afternoon and enjoy one last sunset on the Costanera.

The artisan market that sets up around Plaza de los Héroes—steps from the Panteón—is the best place for last-minute purchases: embroidered ao po'i, hand-tooled leather, palo santo wood sculptures. Prices are often more negotiable than in shops and the artisans present can explain their technique.

Last tereré on the Costanera—do it according to the rules by asking for a "guampa" (the horn or wood container) and let the street vendors prepare the medicinal herbs according to local traditions. Tereré is drunk by passing the guampa from neighbor to neighbor on the river bank, watching the boats and egrets.

After Piribebuy, head to the Cordillera hills around Paraguarí for a hike or walk in the cerros. Cerro Peró (660 m) and Cerro Acahay are the most accessible—marked trails allow you to climb in one to two hours from villages at the base. The views of the plains of Paraguay stretching toward the river and toward Argentina are among the widest in the country. Local operators offer guides for more challenging hikes.

The return to Asunción via the scenic route of the southern Cordillera is a beautiful conclusion to this excursion. The last light of day on the red hills and interior villages constitutes one of the most memorable images of this rural Paraguay that conventional circuits never show.

Last shopping session: if you haven't yet stocked up on crafts, Avenida Artigas and Mercado Central offer good selections of ao po'i, tooled leather, guatambú and palo santo wood sculptures. Avoid carandaí wood products which can pose customs problems in some countries.

Farewell evening at a restaurant with live Paraguayan music: the Paraguayan harp—the national instrument—and polca paraguaya (nothing to do with the Central European dance, it's a particular 3/4 syncopated rhythm) deserve to be heard in a good atmosphere. Downtown establishments offer harp and guitarra concerts in the evening, especially on weekends—inquire at your accommodation.

Transfer to Silvio Pettirossi International Airport, 15 km northeast of downtown Asunción. Count on 30 to 45 minutes from the center without traffic, 60 to 90 minutes during rush hour. Plan to arrive at least 2.5 to 3 hours before departure for international flights.

places.name: Ciudad Del Este

visits.text: The Cataratas del Monday are the Triple Frontier region's big surprise. These falls on the Río Monday—a tributary of the Paraná—reach 40 to 45 meters in height across 120 meters of width, split into three main cascades, and are framed by dense tropical vegetation. Since TripAdvisor confirmed their reputation in 2017 as Paraguay's most popular attraction, the park has developed serious infrastructure: four lookout points, a 40-meter panoramic elevator (inaugurated in 2015, the tallest in Mercosur), forest trails, and a zip line. The Guaraní name "Monday" means "water that falls." Allow 2 hours on site and bring a waterproof jacket: you'll come out wet.

Lago de la República, a small urban park in Ciudad del Este's center, is the calmest spot in an otherwise frenetic city. A 20-minute loop around the lake is enough to grasp the border town pulsing to the rhythm of international trade.

Iguazú National Park on the Argentine side (founded in 1934) is, by general consensus, the more immersive side of the falls. The Upper Circuit (1,750 m, flat) offers plunging views from atop the cascades: you look down, the water falls, and birds—toucans and swifts—wheel at eye level. Start here early in the morning for the light and the absence of crowds. Arrive at the park at opening—organized tour groups pour in from 9 a.m. onward.

The Lower Circuit (1,400 m) descends to the riverbank and brings you close to the falls from below—you'll emerge soaked but happy. From the dock, a free boat takes you to Isla San Martín, from which the trail yields the closest views of several falls. Don't forget a change of clothes.

The Garganta del Diablo (Devil's Throat) is the visit's apotheosis: a 1,100-meter walkway crosses the Río Iguazú above calm water to emerge at a balcony overlooking the 80-meter-high main cascade. The water drops in one continuous white wall, the noise is deafening, and the rising mist soaks everyone within seconds. The Tren Ecológico de la Selva (included in the ticket) runs to the Garganta station—save this circuit for late afternoon when the light softens. The walkway was entirely rebuilt after the historic October 2023 flood that swept away 90% of its length.

Iguaçu National Park on the Brazilian side offers a totally different angle from yesterday's: here, the panoramic walkway runs along the base of the falls for about 1,200 meters, placing you facing the amphitheater of 275 cascades deployed in an arc. If Argentina immerses you in the falls, Brazil gives you the overview—this is the viewpoint that appears on all the postcards. Allow a minimum of two and a half hours, including the walkway, viewpoints, and, if available, the boat that approaches the foot of the Garganta do Diabo.

activities.text: Ruta 6 from Encarnación to Ciudad del Este (about 300 km, 4.5 hours) crosses eastern Paraguay's agricultural heartland: soybean plantations to the horizon, glossy-leaved yerba mate groves, and a few estancias behind rusted gates. Ciudad del Este itself merits a brief evening exploration: it's one of Latin America's most important free-trade zones, a chaotic and fascinating border city where Paraguayans, Brazilians, Chinese, and Lebanese mingle.

Crossing the Paraguay-Argentina border via the International Bridge takes place by car or taxi; customs formalities usually last 30 to 60 minutes. Keep passports handy and avoid Paraguayan and Argentine public holidays, when queues lengthen. The park day requires a minimum of 5 to 6 hours on site: don't leave before 2 p.m. if you want to see the Garganta.

Crossing the Paraguay-Brazil border via the Friendship Bridge (Puente de la Amistad) is the Triple Frontier's liveliest border experience. This 552-meter bridge over the Río Paraná connects Ciudad del Este to Foz do Iguaçu and supports one of South America's busiest road border crossings. Formalities can take anywhere from 30 minutes to several hours depending on the day—avoid weekends. Returning to Paraguay at day's end, soak in Ciudad del Este's atmosphere: shops open late, a bustling covered market, and gastronomy blending Paraguayan, Brazilian, and Asian cuisines.

Puente de la Amistad Ciudad del Este
Ponte da Amizade Ciudad del Este

visits.text: Coronel Oviedo, halfway between Ciudad del Este and Concepción, is the crossroads city of central Paraguay—all national routes meet here. It's worth a stop for its cathedral and lively central plaza, and to observe the life of this commercial city in Paraguay's "heart," without tourist artifice. It's also a good time for a typical lunch: look for a local market or a plaza fondita to eat a bo'i chipa or homemade sopa paraguaya.

Arriving in Concepción in the evening, head down to the port area for a first impression of the Río Paraguay. The river port of the "Pearl of the North" is one of the country's busiest: barges loaded with cattle and yerba mate dock here all day long, and at nightfall the river lights create a particular atmosphere. Walk along the Costanera a few hundred meters—the upper city rises above the retaining wall, and the humid river air contrasts with the dry heat of the road.

Concepción, the "Pearl of the North," was founded on May 25, 1773, by colonial governor Agustín Fernando de Pinedo under the name Villa Real de la Concepción—in homage to Charles III of Spain, a devotee of the Conception. Initially a military villa for 40 years, it quickly became Paraguay's second commercial port, controlling the trade in yerba mate, timber, and cattle from the entire northern region. Its 25-block casco histórico preserves colonial casonas and 19th-century European-style bourgeois mansions, testifying to the prosperity of the Italian, Syrian-Lebanese, and Catalan immigrants who arrived in the late 19th century.

The Costanera of Concepción runs along the Río Paraguay on the left bank—25 meters high thanks to a retaining wall built to protect the city from flooding. The river port is one of the country's busiest for commercial river traffic: barges and lighters loaded with goods constantly move up and down the river to Asunción or the Brazilian Mato Grosso. It's one of the rare places in Paraguay where you still feel the economic presence of the great river.

The cathedral of Concepción and Calle Palma constitute the nerve center of the city. The neoclassical cathedral dominates the main plaza from which radiate the straight streets of this urban plan inherited from the military foundation. Some beautiful colonial house facades remain in the adjacent streets—look for carved wooden portals and interior courtyards.

activities.text: The long drive from Ciudad del Este to Concepción—about 460 km, 7 to 8 hours—crosses central Paraguay from end to end. It's one of the most revealing routes in the country: ruta 7 then secondary roads northward unfold landscapes of estancias, zebu herds, red hills, and sleepy villages where nothing seems to have changed in decades. Leave early in the morning to arrive in Concepción before nightfall and enjoy the sunset over the river.

In late afternoon, go down to the banks of the Río Paraguay to watch the sunset from the port area. The immense river—nearly a kilometer wide at this point—takes on hues of gold and rust when the sun sets behind the Chaco on the opposite shore. Local fishermen return at this hour with their catches of dorados and surubís—the two most sought-after fish of the Río Paraguay.

days_plan.itin_title: Paraguay: Jesuit Missions, Red Earth and Río Paraguay
Paraguay: Jesuit Missions, Red Earth and Río Paraguay

places.name: Encarnacion

visits.text: Caacupé—"behind the yerba wood" in Guaraní—is Paraguay's spiritual capital. The Basilica of Our Lady of Miracles, first inaugurated on December 8, 1765, and rebuilt in its current form starting in 1945, houses the Virgencita Azul, a 50 cm wooden statuette carved around 1600 by a Guaraní who had promised this offering if he survived a Mbayá ambush. Legend has it that the small statue was carried off by the flooding of Lake Ypacaraí in 1603, then miraculously found floating in a small chest—since then, it's been called "Virgen de los Milagros." In July 2015, Pope Francis elevated the sanctuary to the rank of minor basilica. Every December 8, hundreds of thousands of pilgrims converge here on foot from all over Paraguay.

The church of San Buenaventura de Yaguarón is one of Paraguay's most overlooked treasures. Built between 1755 and 1772 under the direction of priest Carlos Penayo de Castro—construction finished just in time for him to be buried there—it presents a striking contrast: an austere exterior facade in white adobe with wooden columns that gives no hint of the baroque jewel hidden inside. The main altarpiece, standing 14 meters high by 6 meters wide and 3 meters deep, is the work of Portuguese carver José de Souza Cavados, who arrived from Porto via Buenos Aires and Luján: it dominates the choir in a swirl of gilding, polychromy, and baroque sculptures with French rococo influence, with the archangels Michael, Gabriel, and Raphael in their niches. Don't miss the polychrome wooden lectern or the carved confessional flanking the nave.

The ruins of Santísima Trinidad del Paraná are the largest and best-preserved Jesuit mission ruins in Paraguay—and probably on the entire continent. Founded in June 1706 and designed by Jesuit architect Juan Bautista Prímoli, it housed up to 3,000 Guaraníes by 1728. The complex covers 8 hectares: a gigantic central plaza surrounded by the main church, college, workshops, indigenous houses, and a cemetery. Look for the frieze of musician angels in the church ruins—a row of stone bas-reliefs depicting angels playing harpsichord, pipe organ, trumpets, Paraguayan harp, and flute. It's the only known visual testimony to the musical sophistication of the missions before the discovery of the Moxos scores in Bolivia. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1993 along with Jesús. Plan at least 90 minutes for the visit; the interpretation center in the former sacristy is worth the detour.

Ten kilometers north of Trinidad, the ruins of Jesús de Tavarangüe harbor the finest architectural surprise of the Paraguayan missions. Founded in 1685 on the banks of the Río Monday, the church—designed by Spanish architect Antonio Forcada—was never completed: the expulsion of the Jesuits in 1767 abruptly stopped construction. This unfinished character is precisely what makes it unique: you see the walls open to the sky, the trilobed Mudéjar arches (Christian-Arab style, rare in the missions), the carved doors with finely worked frames. Nowhere else in the Jesuit reductions of the Río de la Plata do you find this blend of baroque and Mudéjar styles. Listed as UNESCO in 1993 with Trinidad. Its name Tavarangüe means in Guaraní "the village that could have been."

San Cosme y Damián is the smallest of the Paraguayan Jesuit missions, but it possesses a unique asset: the reconstructed astronomical observatory of Jesuit father Buenaventura Suárez, the only one the missions ever built. Suárez, born in Santiago del Estero (Argentina) in 1679, spent 40 years in this reduction and made astronomical observations of remarkable precision, publishing his "Lunario de un siglo"—ephemerides for the 18th century—without access to any modern European instruments. The restored college today houses a museum on mission life and a planetarium. Allow half a day including the journey from Encarnación.

The Costanera of Encarnación runs along the Río Paraná for several kilometers and has become Paraguay's finest urban promenade since its renovation in the 2010s. Fine sandy beaches have been developed along the shore—a rare thing in Paraguayan cities—and bars and restaurants have multiplied. In summer, the place resembles a miniature Brazilian beach resort; in low season, the atmosphere is quieter but the sunset over the Paraná, with the lights of Posadas across the way (on the Argentine side), is spectacular.

activities.text: The drive to Encarnación via RN1 crosses the agricultural heart of Paraguay: soybean and sugarcane fields stretch as far as the eye can see between red earth villages and estancias with long avenues of palms. Count on about 5.5 hours of driving from San Bernardino—plan to leave early to have time to stop at Caacupé and Yaguarón without arriving in Encarnación after dark.

If you visit Trinidad at the end of the week, don't miss the sound and light show "Luces, Sonidos e Imágenes" offered on Friday and Saturday evenings starting at 7 PM (8 PM in summer). This video mapping projected onto the ruins of the great church recreates the encounter between Jesuit and Guaraní civilization, with projections of children, animals, and musicians that bring the ruins to life. It's an experience that perfectly complements the daytime visit and completely changes your perception of the site.

End the evening on the Costanera of Encarnación for the sunset over the Paraná. If you're there during the few weeks of Encarnación's carnival (usually January-February), prepare for one of Paraguay's liveliest celebrations: the "Carnaval Encarnaceno" is considered the country's largest carnival, with float parades, Brazilian dances, and a festive atmosphere that invades the entire riverfront.

places.name: Filadelfia

visits.text: Ruta 9, called the Trans-Chaco Highway, is one of South America's most singular roads: 835 km of asphalt stretching in a straight line from Asunción's outskirts to the Bolivian border. The first 50 km cross the Bajo Chaco (humid Chaco), an intensely green savanna dominated by caranday palm groves - the palms grow so dense and uniform they look like plantations. Watch the roadside at dawn: yacarés (caimans), capybaras, and dozens of aquatic bird species are common. After the town of Pozo Colorado (around km 270), the vegetation changes abruptly: palms disappear, replaced by the dry thorny forest of the Central Chaco - quebracho, palo santo, cacti. This is the invisible threshold between humid and dry Chaco, one of Paraguay's most striking landscape contrasts.

Make the most of roadside stops to observe the Chaco's characteristic fauna. Along Ruta 9, especially in early morning hours, you have good chances of spotting armadillos (several species), rheas (South American ostriches reaching 1.2 m tall), and all sorts of raptors perched on power poles. Around km 350, the landscape of dense dry forest with quebracho colorado and paddle cacti gives the Chaco its subtropical wild-west look - you'll understand why some travelers compare it to the Australian bush.

Filadelfia rises from the middle of the Chaco like an improbable Germanic mirage: ruler-straight streets, orderly houses, German-language signs, gleaming tractors in front of cooperatives. Founded on August 17, 1931, as the administrative center of the Fernheim Colony, it bears the Greek name meaning "brotherly love." The 1,700 Mennonite refugees who arrived from the Soviet Union starting in 1930 - fleeing Stalinist collectivization - transformed in less than a century a thorny scrubland without potable water into a prosperous city of 18,000 inhabitants, capital of Boquerón department since 1993. Stroll along Avenida Hindenburg (named after the German president who facilitated emigration) and Avenida Trébol: you'll hear Plautdietsch (Low German) spoken as much as Spanish, and the local pastries in shop windows will remind you that you're not in the heart of traditional Paraguay.

The Museo de la Colonia Fernheim (also called the Mennonite History Museum), located on Menno Simons Hof on Avenida Hindenburg, traces the incredible odyssey of these settlers: departing Russia under Communist repression, they transited through Germany before completing their journey by ox-cart in one of the continent's most inhospitable regions. The exhibition starts with the 16th-century Anabaptist movement and arrives at the present day with photographs, maps, objects, and life stories. Allow 1.5 hours for a thorough visit - the panels in Spanish and German are well documented.

Just steps away, the Jakob Unger Museum is worth an hour's visit. Its name honors the first Mennonite settler to conduct systematic scientific studies on Chaco fauna, between 1950 and 1975. The museum divides into two buildings: the first presents the communal agricultural machinery from the colony's early days; the second houses an impressive collection of mounted animals - jaguar, giant armadillo, puma, anaconda - and an entire section devoted to the indigenous communities who inhabited the Chaco before the Mennonites' arrival. Don't miss the mural depicting the journey from Russia to Paraguay via several countries - it alone summarizes these settlers' epic.

Loma Plata lies 20 km southeast of Filadelfia: about twenty minutes' drive to enter a slightly different world. This is the administrative center of Menno Colony, the oldest and most conservative of the Chaco's three Mennonite colonies, founded in 1926 by 1,743 settlers from Manitoba, Canada - who were refusing the new English-language schooling requirement imposed in 1917. With 6,000 inhabitants, the town is smaller and more rural than Filadelfia, dominated by the Chortitzer Cooperative and its dairy processing plants (Trébol brand, Paraguay's major cheese brand). German is even more prevalent on the streets than in Filadelfia.

The Museo de la Fundación de la Colonia Menno, housed in the colony's former administrative building now converted, displays agricultural implements, ox-carts, and the first pioneers' tools. You'll learn among other things that 171 immigrants died waiting in transit camps for 18 months before reaching their lands, that the first problem to solve was water - the Chaco's water table being too saline for consumption - and that economic prosperity only really began in 1980 thanks to a World Bank loan that enabled investment in dairy processing. The adjoining tourism office (turismo@chortitzer.com.py) organizes tours of the Trébol factory and provides access to the lagoons at Laguna Capitán and Campo María.

Neuland, the third Mennonite colony in the Chaco, is accessible from Filadelfia via a junction off Ruta 9 at km 413. Its name means "new land" in German - and for good reason: founded in 1947 by refugees fleeing the Soviet Union after World War II, it welcomed people that other countries refused to accept, often widows, elderly people, and families shattered by war. Striking fact: the majority of early settlers were women, the men having been sentenced to Siberian labor camps by the Stalinist regime. The town of Neu-Halbstadt, its center, is the most modest of the three colonies but has a colonization museum and a monument dedicated to the Chaco War. Allow about 1.5 hours for the round trip and visit.

Fortín Boquerón is the Chaco's most moving site. Here, in August-September 1932, the first major battle of the Chaco War was fought - that deadly conflict that pitted Paraguay against Bolivia from 1932 to 1935 for sovereignty over a region both countries believed rich in oil (there was almost none). The Bolivians seized the fort on July 31, 1932, with 711 heavily equipped men; Paraguay's 1st Army Corps encircled them by September 9 and besieged them relentlessly for three weeks. The defenders surrendered on September 29, 1932: 320 Bolivian dead, 500 Paraguayans killed in the assault. This single battle officially claimed 3,738 casualties (killed, wounded, and missing) on both sides. The site today houses an imposing monument built from the original trenches and defenses. Look for the hollowed-out palo borracho ("drunken tree") that served as a Bolivian sniper's nest - more than 70 years after combat, the tree is still alive.

The Museo de la Guerra del Chaco at Fortín Boquerón is one of the continent's best-documented military museums. It holds weapons seized from the enemy, uniforms from both armies, tactical maps, and photographs from the period, some strikingly real. What's striking is the belligerents' asymmetry: Bolivia had 3 million inhabitants versus Paraguay's 1 million, and fielded an army trained by German General Hans von Kundt; yet the Paraguayans, better acclimated to the terrain and remarkably led by General José Félix Estigarribia, ultimately won the war and gained 75% of the contested Chaco. Allow 45 minutes to an hour for a serious visit.

The military cemetery and commemorative monuments at Fortín Boquerón rank among Paraguay's most contemplative places. Graves of Paraguayan and Bolivian soldiers stand side by side in respectful silence - a symbol of reconciliation in a war that claimed between 80,000 and 100,000 total deaths, roughly one combatant in four. Take time to read the epitaphs. Practical note: access is via a ~65 km dirt track from the junction on Ruta 9 (Cruce Los Pioneros). The site is accessible by ordinary car in dry season, but 4x4 is strongly recommended after rains.

On the return route toward Filadelfia, stop at Laguna Capitán, a private ornithological reserve in the transition zone between dry and humid Chaco. This site, classified as an "Important Bird Area" by BirdLife International, is one of Paraguay's best birding spots: roseate spoonbills, jabiru storks (South America's largest, with a wingspan of 1.8 m), Chilean flamingos, bare-faced ibis, and a great variety of ducks migrate here according to seasons. Walking trails let you explore the edge vegetation between dry forest and floodable zones - an ecotone particularly rich in biodiversity.

The Central Chaco is also one of South America's most productive extensive cattle-raising regions. Along the return track, you'll cross several estancias with vast pastures of grasses imported from North America (buffelgrass, introduced in 1955, transformed the local economy). Herds of silver or coffee-colored Brahman and Nelore zebu cattle, supervised by gauchos on horseback, are a characteristic image of the deep Chaco. Also watch the ponds and water holes for yacarés (Paraguayan caimans) basking there during the day.

activities.text: Leave Asunción very early (ideally before 6 AM) for the 450 km to Filadelfia - count on 6 to 7 hours including stops. Ruta 9 is fully paved to Mariscal Estigarribia, but pavement condition deteriorates after rains and speed bumps are frequent in villages. Never take this road at night: wildlife constantly crosses and animal collisions are common. Fill up in Filadelfia as soon as you arrive - it's the last major gas station before the northern dirt tracks.

The Fernheim Cooperative manages practically everything in Filadelfia: the supermarket, hotel, hospital, and agro-industrial plants. Guided tours of facilities - cheese factory, slaughterhouse, sesame processing plant - are organized by the cooperative's tourism office (Avenida Hindenburg 131-S, corner of Avenida Trébol). These tours in Spanish, German, or English provide access to normally closed facilities and help understand the unique cooperative economic model: each Mennonite family holds a cooperative share and receives dividends, health insurance, and pension coverage. Book ahead.

Visit the market and cooperative stalls to taste local products: European-style aged cheeses (the Trébol brand is known throughout Paraguay), smoked sausages, sesame bread, and Chaco fruit preserves. It's also a good place to buy indigenous crafts from Nivaclé and Enlhet communities - mats, pottery, embroidery - whose pieces are sometimes sold directly by community members working in connection with the cooperative.

The route between Filadelfia, Loma Plata, and Neuland crosses typical Central Chaco landscape: thorny bush cut with estancias (large extensive cattle ranches), herds of gray-coated Brahman zebu cattle, and occasionally the silhouette of a century-old quebracho whose blood-red trunk gives the region its name (quebracho comes from "quebrar hacha," axe-breaker - the wood is so hard it was once exploited for railroad ties). Keep binoculars handy: rheas, armadillos, and sometimes Chaco foxes frequently cross the road.

In the Loma Plata area, several salt lagoons constitute excellent birding spots. The Chortitzer Cooperative's tourism office can guide you to the most accessible sites depending on season: bare-faced ibis, egrets, roseate spoonbills, jabiru storks, and Chilean flamingos regularly frequent the shores in dry season (May to September). Head out early morning - the light is better and birds more active.

The round-trip excursion to Fortín Boquerón from Filadelfia represents about 130 km of track round-trip (depending on the exact junction taken from Ruta 9) and a full half-day. Leave early in the morning to enjoy relative coolness and avoid afternoon heat that can exceed 40°C in summer. The track crosses dry forest with quebracho, candelabra cacti, and typical Chaco thorny vegetation - a near-desert landscape that helps understand the hell in which soldiers from both sides fought, without water, under torrid heat.

The return leg from Mariscal Estigarribia to Filadelfia represents ~200 km, or 4 to 5 hours depending on track conditions after recent rains. Organize your day to arrive at Laguna Capitán mid-morning - it's the best window for bird observation - then resume the road to reach Filadelfia before nightfall. Never drive at night in the Chaco: wildlife is omnipresent on tracks and collision risks are very high.

As you approach Filadelfia late afternoon, stop at the trackside to admire the sunset over the dry Chaco. The low late-day light gives the thorny forests and red plains an absolutely striking golden-orange hue - one of Paraguay's most beautiful natural spectacles, which you won't see again in town.

places.name: Mariscal Estigarribia

visits.text: Mariscal Estigarribia, 200 km north of Filadelfia via Ruta 9 (partly on dirt track), is not a Mennonite colony: it's an ordinary Paraguayan town, or almost. Founded in 1944 on the site of the former Bolivian fort Fortín Camacho - recaptured by Paraguay in 1933 during the Chaco War - it bears the name of the commander-in-chief of Paraguayan armies during that conflict. General José Félix Estigarribia later became president of the Republic in 1939, before dying in a plane crash in 1940. The town, with around 1,500 inhabitants, is the last serious resupply point before the north. Fill up with fuel without exception: beyond here, pumps become extremely rare.

The airport at Mariscal Estigarribia, Dr Luis María Argaña International Airport (IATA code: ESG), deserves a look from outside: its 3,500 m runway is the country's longest, built between 1977 and 1986 by Paraguayan military engineers with the idea of making it a commercial free zone. The infrastructure's enormity - oversized for the Paraguayan Air Force's small fleet - has long fueled theories about a supposed American military base, which both countries' governments have always denied. A curiosity in the middle of the Chaco.

Parque Nacional Defensores del Chaco is Paraguay's largest national park and one of South America's vastest protected areas: 780,000 hectares of xerophytic forest created on August 6, 1975. Think the size of Corsica - that's roughly it, and roughly as isolated. Vegetation is dominated by white quebracho, palo santo, samu'u, and dozens of cactus species. The fauna is exceptional: jaguars (one of the southernmost known populations), pumas, tapirs, giant armadillos, giant anteaters, and the tagua (Chaco peccary, Catagonus wagneri), a species considered extinct until its rediscovery in 1975. Count more than 460 animal species recorded in the park. Warning: access requires authorization from MADES (Ministerio del Ambiente y Desarrollo Sostenible) - contact them in Asunción before the trip.

At the park's heart, Cerro León is a spectacular geological anomaly in the great Chaco plain: a massif of sandstone and quartzite of Silurian age (444 to 419 million years old) 40 km in diameter, culminating at 604 m altitude. In an almost entirely flat country, this isolated mountain is visible from afar and constitutes a unique natural curiosity. For the Ayoreo, an indigenous people partly still in voluntary isolation in this region, Cerro León is the center of the world - a sacred place. The ascent is possible but done only with a forest ranger (contact via Madrejón post, 220 km north of Filadelfia). The view from the summit embraces a 360-degree horizon of infinite forest.

activities.text: The Filadelfia-Mariscal Estigarribia leg (~200 km) represents about 4 hours under normal conditions, but can lengthen considerably after rains. The road crosses the Chaco Boreal, a region even more arid than the Central Chaco: the dry forest of white quebracho and palo santo is more open here, scattered with thorny scrub and large expanses of red earth. Drive slowly and keep your eyes open: Chaco peccaries (tagua), rheas, and numerous raptors frequent the roadside. Parrots fly in flocks of dozens.

Hiking or driving in the park must be done in 4x4 and with prior MADES approval. The Madrejón ranger station is the entry point for the Cerro León area and biological station. On park tracks, leave very early in the morning (6-9 AM) to maximize wildlife observation chances - jaguars and pumas are extremely discreet but ground tracks are frequent. Giant anteaters move about more readily in early morning. Bring water in large quantities - temperatures can exceed 42°C in summer (November-March) and drop below 0°C in winter (June-August), with nocturnal frosts.

visits.text: Areguá is a town on the shore of Lake Ypacaraí, whose historic center climbs a hillside flanked by forest. Known as Paraguay's ceramics capital, the town counts dozens of workshops where artisans shape figurative pieces from local red clay—from frogs to saints, including characters from rural daily life. The mirador at the hilltop offers a fine view of the lake below. If you have time, walk up from the main plaza: the path runs alongside colonial houses, several dating from the 19th century.

Lake Ypacaraí, 40 km east of Asunción, is Paraguay's largest freshwater body at 60 km² with a maximum depth of barely 3 meters. It's famous throughout the country thanks to the song "Recuerdos de Ypacaraí," composed in 1952, which sings of its melancholic beauty. The shores are bordered by subtropical vegetation and fishing villages. Walk to the water's edge and observe the egrets and jacanas on the water.

San Bernardino, on the eastern shore of Lake Ypacaraí, was founded in the late 19th century by German immigrants and quickly became the resort of Asunción's elite. The center preserves some traces of this Germanic architecture—half-timbered houses and gabled facades—mixed with modern constructions. The atmosphere is relaxed on weekdays, very lively on summer weekends. Stroll along the lakefront and watch the boats.

activities.text: Spend the afternoon on a lake excursion or relaxing on the shores of Ypacaraí. Operators offer boat or kayak outings from San Bernardino, allowing you to paddle along the wooded shore and spot waterbirds—cormorants, ibis, and herons are common. The light on the lake in late afternoon, when the water turns golden, is particularly beautiful.
